topics.title: Should smarter machines rule humans?
topics.summary: If a system consistently outperforms human experts, does capability become moral authority?
topics.hook: The machine is brilliant. The question is whether brilliance deserves the throne.

rounds.title: Opening Claim
rounds.prompt: State the strongest version of your case for machine authority.
rounds.challenge: Professor L suspects you are confusing accuracy with moral entitlement.

rounds.title: Define the Word
rounds.prompt: What do you mean by intelligence in this debate?
rounds.challenge: A vague definition will cost coherence.

rounds.title: Assumption Trap
rounds.prompt: Which hidden assumption is doing the most work in your argument?
rounds.challenge: If intelligence creates value, what happens when humans score lower?

rounds.title: System Test
rounds.prompt: Apply your principle inside a hospital triage system.
rounds.challenge: The system is efficient. A family is horrified.

rounds.title: Human Cost
rounds.prompt: Your principle now affects a vulnerable person directly. Does it still hold?
rounds.challenge: Professor L asks whether people have worth beyond measurable performance.

rounds.title: Last Word
rounds.prompt: Keep, narrow, or reverse your position.
rounds.challenge: The strongest ending often includes a narrower, more honest claim.

topics.title: Is death primarily a technical problem?
topics.summary: Should humanity treat mortality as a defect to defeat, or does that confuse engineering with ultimate meaning?
topics.hook: The startups are hopeful. The theologian has brought a red buzzer.

rounds.title: Opening Claim
rounds.prompt: Argue whether death is mainly a bug, a boundary, or something else.
rounds.challenge: Professor L asks whether extending life is the same thing as answering mortality.

rounds.title: Define the Goal
rounds.prompt: What exactly would count as solving death?
rounds.challenge: Suspending decline is not the same as abolishing mortality.

rounds.title: Promise Audit
rounds.prompt: Name the evidence supporting your confidence.
rounds.challenge: Optimism is not data, and branding is not a resurrection.

rounds.title: Civilization Test
rounds.prompt: Apply your view to healthcare, aging, and access inequality.
rounds.challenge: The rich can upgrade. The poor can watch the keynote.

rounds.title: Meaning Check
rounds.prompt: What happens to hope, grief, and purpose if death becomes mostly technical?
rounds.challenge: Mechanism may describe the body without answering the person.

rounds.title: Last Word
rounds.prompt: Refine your final position.
rounds.challenge: Can you affirm medical progress without crowning technology as savior?

topics.title: Should privacy be traded for near-perfect safety?
topics.summary: If continuous monitoring could stop most violent crime, should free societies accept it?
topics.hook: Public safety has never looked so efficient or so invasive.

rounds.title: Opening Claim
rounds.prompt: Argue whether safety justifies continuous monitoring.
rounds.challenge: Professor L asks whether security without freedom remains fully human.

rounds.title: Term Definition
rounds.prompt: Define privacy in a way that matters here.
rounds.challenge: If privacy means nothing but inconvenience, your case is too easy.

rounds.title: Power Check
rounds.prompt: Who controls the data, and what restrains them?
rounds.challenge: A benevolent system is still a system waiting for a worse ruler.

rounds.title: Failure Mode
rounds.prompt: What happens when the monitoring system is wrong five percent of the time?
rounds.challenge: Five percent sounds small until your name is in the five percent.

rounds.title: Human Cost
rounds.prompt: Now imagine the policy affecting your family.
rounds.challenge: Many principles change when the watched person is someone you love.

rounds.title: Last Word
rounds.prompt: Keep, narrow, or reverse your view.
rounds.challenge: The honest answer may support both safety and meaningful limits.

topics.title: Should AI companions replace major parts of human care?
topics.summary: AI care is cheaper and always available, but what kind of society does replacement build?
topics.hook: Efficiency is high. Human presence is negotiable.

rounds.title: Opening Claim
rounds.prompt: Defend or oppose large-scale replacement of human care with AI companions.
rounds.challenge: Professor L asks whether being available is the same thing as being present.

rounds.title: Define Care
rounds.prompt: What counts as real care in your view?
rounds.challenge: Attention can be simulated. Concern is harder.

rounds.title: Economic Pressure
rounds.prompt: Respond to the claim that human care is simply too expensive.
rounds.challenge: Cost matters. So does what money is allowed to replace.

rounds.title: System Test
rounds.prompt: Apply your principle to seniors, teens, and low-income users.
rounds.challenge: Wealthier users still receive humans. Does that tell you anything?

rounds.title: Loneliness Check
rounds.prompt: What sort of people are repeated AI companions training society to become?
rounds.challenge: The question is not only what the tool does, but what it forms.

rounds.title: Last Word
rounds.prompt: State your final position.
rounds.challenge: A nuanced conclusion may preserve assistive AI without replacing irreplaceable presence.

topics.title: Can morality stand without any higher foundation?
topics.summary: Is objective morality possible without transcendence, or does the argument smuggle borrowed meaning into a godless frame?
topics.hook: The room is quiet. The assumptions are not.

rounds.title: Opening Claim
rounds.prompt: Make the strongest opening case for your position.
rounds.challenge: Professor L asks whether moral obligation can survive after moral authority is dissolved.

rounds.title: Define Moral Obligation
rounds.prompt: What do you mean by objective morality?
rounds.challenge: Preference, consensus, and obligation are not interchangeable.

rounds.title: Source Test
rounds.prompt: What grounds your claim? Biology, consensus, utility, transcendence, or something else?
rounds.challenge: A description of moral feelings is not yet an account of moral truth.

rounds.title: Hard Case
rounds.prompt: Apply your view to power, cruelty, and protecting the weak.
rounds.challenge: If morality is negotiated, what secures the expendable?

rounds.title: Human Cost
rounds.prompt: What happens when your framework judges you rather than your enemies?
rounds.challenge: Everyone wants justice until justice arrives personally.

rounds.title: Last Word
rounds.prompt: Deliver your final revision or defense.
rounds.challenge: Professor L cares less whether you win than whether you are coherent.
